speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: Once, if he do require our voices, we

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: ought not to deny him.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: We may, sir, if we will.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: We have power in ourselves to do it, but

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: it is a power that we have no power to do; for, if

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: he show us his wounds and tell us his deeds, we

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: are to put our tongues into those wounds and

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: speak for them. So, if he tell us his noble deeds, we

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: must also tell him our noble acceptance of them.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Ingratitude is monstrous, and for the multitude to

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: be ingrateful were to make a monster of the multitude,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: of the which, we being members, should

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: bring ourselves to be monstrous members.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: And to make us no better thought of, a

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: little help will serve; for once we stood up about

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: the corn, he himself stuck not to call us the many-headed

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: multitude.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: We have been called so of many; not that

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: our heads are some brown, some black, some

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: abram, some bald, but that our wits are so diversely

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: colored; and truly I think if all our wits were to

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: issue out of one skull, they would fly east, west,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: north, south, and their consent of one direct way

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: should be at once to all the points o’ th’ compass.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: Think you so? Which way do you

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: judge my wit would fly?

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Nay, your wit will not so soon out as another

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: man’s will; ’tis strongly wedged up in a blockhead.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: But if it were at liberty, ’twould sure

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: southward.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: Why that way?

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: To lose itself in a fog, where, being three

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: parts melted away with rotten dews, the fourth

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: would return for conscience’ sake, to help to get

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: thee a wife.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: You are never without your tricks. You

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: may, you may.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Are you all resolved to give your voices?

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: But that’s no matter; the greater part carries it. I

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: say, if he would incline to the people, there was

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: never a worthier man.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Here he comes, and in the gown of humility. Mark

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: his behavior. We are not to stay all together, but to

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: come by him where he stands, by ones, by twos,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: and by threes. He’s to make his requests by particulars,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: wherein every one of us has a single honor

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: in giving him our own voices with our own tongues.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Therefore follow me, and I’ll direct you how you

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: shall go by him.

speaker: ALL
text: Content, content.

speaker: MENENIUS
text: O sir, you are not right. Have you not known

speaker: MENENIUS
text: The worthiest men have done ’t?

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: What must I say?

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: “I pray, sir?”—plague upon ’t! I cannot bring

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: My tongue to such a pace. “Look, sir, my wounds!

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: I got them in my country’s service when

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Some certain of your brethren roared and ran

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: From th’ noise of our own drums.”

speaker: MENENIUS
text: O me, the gods!

speaker: MENENIUS
text: You must not speak of that. You must desire them

speaker: MENENIUS
text: To think upon you.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Think upon me? Hang ’em!

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: I would they would forget me, like the virtues

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Which our divines lose by ’em.

speaker: MENENIUS
text: You’ll mar all.

speaker: MENENIUS
text: I’ll leave you. Pray you, speak to ’em, I pray you,

speaker: MENENIUS
text: In wholesome manner.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Bid them wash their faces

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: And keep their teeth clean.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: So, here comes a brace.—

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: You know the cause, sir, of my standing here.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: We do, sir. Tell us what hath brought you to ’t.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Mine own desert.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: Your own desert?

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Ay, but not mine own desire.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: How, not your own desire?

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: No, sir, ’twas never my desire yet to trouble

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: the poor with begging.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: You must think if we give you anything,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: we hope to gain by you.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Well then, I pray, your price o’ th’

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: consulship?

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: The price is to ask it kindly.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Kindly, sir, I pray, let me ha ’t. I have

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: wounds to show you, which shall be yours in

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: private.—Your good voice, sir. What say you?

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: You shall ha ’t, worthy sir.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: A match, sir. There’s in all two worthy

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: voices begged. I have your alms. Adieu.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: But this is something

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: odd.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: An ’twere to give again—but ’tis no

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: matter.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Pray you now, if it may stand with the

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: tune of your voices that I may be consul, I have

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: here the customary gown.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: You have deserved nobly of your

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: country, and you have not deserved nobly.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Your enigma?

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: You have been a scourge to her enemies;

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: you have been a rod to her friends. You have

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: not indeed loved the common people.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: You should account me the more virtuous

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: that I have not been common in my love. I will, sir,

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: flatter my sworn brother, the people, to earn a

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: dearer estimation of them; ’tis a condition they account

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: gentle. And since the wisdom of their choice

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: is rather to have my hat than my heart, I will practice

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: the insinuating nod and be off to them most

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: counterfeitly. That is, sir, I will counterfeit the bewitchment

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: of some popular man and give it bountiful

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: to the desirers. Therefore, beseech you, I may

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: be consul.

speaker:
FIFTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: We hope to find you our friend, and

speaker:
FIFTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: therefore give you our voices heartily.

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: You have received many wounds for

speaker:
FOURTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: your country.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: I will not seal your knowledge with showing

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: them. I will make much of your voices and so

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: trouble you no farther.

speaker: BOTH
text: The gods give you joy, sir, heartily.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Most sweet voices!

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Better it is to die, better to starve,

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Than crave the hire which first we do deserve.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Why in this woolvish toge should I stand here

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: To beg of Hob and Dick that does appear

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Their needless vouches? Custom calls me to ’t.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: What custom wills, in all things should we do ’t?

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: The dust on antique time would lie unswept

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: And mountainous error be too highly heaped

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: For truth to o’erpeer. Rather than fool it so,

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Let the high office and the honor go

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: To one that would do thus. I am half through;

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: The one part suffered, the other will I do.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Here come more voices.—

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Your voices! For your voices I have fought;

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Watched for your voices; for your voices bear

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Of wounds two dozen odd. Battles thrice six

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: I have seen and heard of; for your voices have

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Done many things, some less, some more. Your voices!

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Indeed, I would be consul.

speaker:
SIXTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: He has done nobly, and cannot go

speaker:
SIXTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: without any honest man’s voice.

speaker:
SEVENTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: Therefore let him be consul. The

speaker:
SEVENTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: gods give him joy, and make him good friend to

speaker:
SEVENTH
               
              CITIZEN
text: the people!

speaker: ALL
text: Amen, amen. God save thee, noble consul.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Worthy voices!

speaker: MENENIUS
text: You have stood your limitation, and the Tribunes

speaker: MENENIUS
text: Endue you with the people’s voice. Remains

speaker: MENENIUS
text: That in th’ official marks invested, you

speaker: MENENIUS
text: Anon do meet the Senate.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Is this done?

speaker: SICINIUS
text: The custom of request you have discharged.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: The people do admit you, and are summoned

speaker: SICINIUS
text: To meet anon upon your approbation.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Where? At the Senate House?

speaker: SICINIUS
text: There, Coriolanus.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: May I change these garments?

speaker: SICINIUS
text: You may, sir.

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: That I’ll straight do and, knowing myself again,

speaker: CORIOLANUS
text: Repair to th’ Senate House.

speaker: MENENIUS
text: I’ll keep you company.—Will you along?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: We stay here for the people.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Fare you well.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: He has it now; and by his looks, methinks,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: ’Tis warm at ’s heart.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: With a proud heart he wore

speaker: BRUTUS
text: His humble weeds. Will you dismiss the people?

speaker: SICINIUS
text: How now, my masters, have you chose this man?

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: He has our voices, sir.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: We pray the gods he may deserve your loves.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: Amen, sir. To my poor unworthy notice,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: He mocked us when he begged our voices.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Certainly, he flouted us downright.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: No, ’tis his kind of speech. He did not mock us.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: Not one amongst us, save yourself, but says

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: He used us scornfully. He should have showed us

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: His marks of merit, wounds received for ’s country.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Why, so he did, I am sure.

speaker: ALL
text: No, no. No man saw ’em.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: He said he had wounds, which he could show in private,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: And with his hat, thus waving it in scorn,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: “I would be consul,” says he. “Agèd custom,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: But by your voices, will not so permit me;

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Your voices therefore.” When we granted that,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Here was “I thank you for your voices. Thank you.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: Your most sweet voices! Now you have left your voices,

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: I have no further with you.” Was not this mockery?

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Why either were you ignorant to see ’t

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Or, seeing it, of such childish friendliness

speaker: SICINIUS
text: To yield your voices?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Could you not have told him

speaker: BRUTUS
text: As you were lessoned? When he had no power,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But was a petty servant to the state,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: He was your enemy, ever spake against

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Your liberties and the charters that you bear

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I’ th’ body of the weal; and, now arriving

speaker: BRUTUS
text: A place of potency and sway o’ th’ state,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: If he should still malignantly remain

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Fast foe to th’ plebeii, your voices might

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Be curses to yourselves. You should have said

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That as his worthy deeds did claim no less

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Than what he stood for, so his gracious nature

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Would think upon you for your voices, and

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Translate his malice towards you into love,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Standing your friendly lord.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Thus to have said,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: As you were fore-advised, had touched his spirit

speaker: SICINIUS
text: And tried his inclination; from him plucked

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Either his gracious promise, which you might,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: As cause had called you up, have held him to;

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Or else it would have galled his surly nature,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Which easily endures not article

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Tying him to aught. So putting him to rage,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: You should have ta’en th’ advantage of his choler

speaker: SICINIUS
text: And passed him unelected.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Did you perceive

speaker: BRUTUS
text: He did solicit you in free contempt

speaker: BRUTUS
text: When he did need your loves, and do you think

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That his contempt shall not be bruising to you

speaker: BRUTUS
text: When he hath power to crush? Why, had your bodies

speaker: BRUTUS
text: No heart among you? Or had you tongues to cry

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Against the rectorship of judgment?

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Have you ere now denied the asker? And now

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Again, of him that did not ask but mock,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Bestow your sued-for tongues?

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: He’s not confirmed.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              CITIZEN
text: We may deny him yet.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: And will deny him.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              CITIZEN
text: I’ll have five hundred voices of that sound.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              CITIZEN
text: I twice five hundred, and their friends to piece ’em.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Get you hence instantly, and tell those friends

speaker: BRUTUS
text: They have chose a consul that will from them take

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Their liberties, make them of no more voice

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Than dogs that are as often beat for barking

speaker: BRUTUS
text: As therefor kept to do so.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Let them assemble

speaker: SICINIUS
text: And, on a safer judgment, all revoke

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Your ignorant election. Enforce his pride

speaker: SICINIUS
text: And his old hate unto you. Besides, forget not

speaker: SICINIUS
text: With what contempt he wore the humble weed,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: How in his suit he scorned you; but your loves,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Thinking upon his services, took from you

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Th’ apprehension of his present portance,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Which most gibingly, ungravely, he did fashion

speaker: SICINIUS
text: After the inveterate hate he bears you.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Lay

speaker: BRUTUS
text: A fault on us, your tribunes, that we labored,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: No impediment between, but that you must

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Cast your election on him.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Say you chose him

speaker: SICINIUS
text: More after our commandment than as guided

speaker: SICINIUS
text: By your own true affections, and that your minds,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Preoccupied with what you rather must do

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Than what you should, made you against the grain

speaker: SICINIUS
text: To voice him consul. Lay the fault on us.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Ay, spare us not. Say we read lectures to you,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: How youngly he began to serve his country,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: How long continued, and what stock he springs of,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The noble house o’ th’ Martians, from whence came

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That Ancus Martius, Numa’s daughter’s son,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Who after great Hostilius here was king,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Of the same house Publius and Quintus were,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That our best water brought by conduits hither;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And Censorinus, that was so surnamed,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And nobly namèd so, twice being censor,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Was his great ancestor.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: One thus descended,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: That hath besides well in his person wrought

speaker: SICINIUS
text: To be set high in place, we did commend

speaker: SICINIUS
text: To your remembrances; but you have found,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Scaling his present bearing with his past,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: That he’s your fixèd enemy, and revoke

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Your sudden approbation.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Say you ne’er had done ’t—

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Harp on that still—but by our putting on.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And presently, when you have drawn your number,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Repair to th’ Capitol.

speaker: ALL
text: We will so. Almost all

speaker: ALL
text: Repent in their election.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Let them go on.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: This mutiny were better put in hazard

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Than stay, past doubt, for greater.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: If, as his nature is, he fall in rage

speaker: BRUTUS
text: With their refusal, both observe and answer

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The vantage of his anger.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: To th’ Capitol, come.

speaker: SICINIUS
text: We will be there before the stream o’ th’ people,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: And this shall seem, as partly ’tis, their own,

speaker: SICINIUS
text: Which we have goaded onward.
